# Wearable Devices for Supporting Chronic Disease Self-Management: Scoping Review

## Wearable Devices for Supporting Chronic Disease Self-Management: Scoping Review

Authors of this article:

![Author Orcid Image](https://asset.jmir.pub/assets/static/images/Orcid-ID-Logo-Colour.png)
![Author Orcid Image](https://asset.jmir.pub/assets/static/images/Orcid-ID-Logo-Colour.png)

#### Review

1Faculty of Nursing Sciences, Université Laval, Québec, QC, Canada

2VITAM Research Center on Sustainable Health, Québec, QC, Canada

3School of Nutrition, Université Laval, Québec, QC, Canada

4Department of Family Medicine and Emergency Medicine, Faculty of Medicine, Université Laval, Québec, QC, Canada

5Research Center of Quebec Heart and Lungs Institute, Québec, QC, Canada

6Patient Partner, VITAM Research Center on Sustainable Health, Québec, QC, Canada

7Library, Université Laval, Québec, QC, Canada

### Corresponding Author:

Marie-Pierre Gagnon, PhD

Faculty of Nursing Sciences

Université Laval

1050 Av. de la Médecine

Québec, QC, G1V 0A6

Canada

Phone: 1 418 656 2131 ext 407576

Email: [marie-pierre.gagnon@fsi.ulaval.ca](mailto:marie-pierre.gagnon@fsi.ulaval.ca)

### Abstract

Background: People with chronic diseases can benefit from wearable devices in managing their health and encouraging healthy lifestyle habits. Wearables such as activity trackers or blood glucose monitoring devices can lead to positive health impacts, including improved physical activity adherence or better management of type 2 diabetes. Few literature reviews have focused on the intersection of various chronic diseases, the wearable devices used, and the outcomes evaluated in intervention studies, particularly in the context of primary health care.

Objective: This study aims to identify and describe (1) the chronic diseases represented in intervention studies, (2) the types or combinations of wearables used, and (3) the health or health care outcomes assessed and measured.

Methods: We conducted a scoping review following the Joanna Briggs Institute guidelines, searching the MEDLINE and Web of Science databases for studies published between 2012 and 2022. Pairs of reviewers independently screened titles and abstracts, applied the selection criteria, and performed full-text screening. We included interventions using wearables that automatically collected and transmitted data to adult populations with at least one chronic disease. We excluded studies with participants with only a predisposition to develop a chronic disease, hospitalized patients, patients with acute diseases, patients with active cancer, and cancer survivors. We included randomized controlled trials and cohort, pretest-posttest, observational, mixed methods, and qualitative studies.

Results: After the removal of 1987 duplicates, we screened 4540 titles and abstracts. Of the remaining 304 articles after exclusions, we excluded 215 (70.7%) full texts and included 89 (29.3%). Of these 89 texts, 10 (11%) were related to the same interventions as those in the included studies, resulting in 79 studies being included. We structured the results according to chronic disease clusters: (1) diabetes, (2) heart failure, (3) other cardiovascular conditions, (4) hypertension, (5) multimorbidity and other combinations of chronic conditions, (6) chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, (7) chronic pain, (8) musculoskeletal conditions, and (9) asthma. Diabetes was the most frequent health condition (18/79, 23% of the studies), and wearable activity trackers were the most used (42/79, 53% of the studies). In the 79 included studies, 74 clinical, 73 behavioral, 36 patient technology experience, 28 health care system, and 25 holistic or biopsychosocial outcomes were reported.

Conclusions: This scoping review provides an overview of the wearable devices used in chronic disease self-management intervention studies, revealing disparities in both the range of chronic diseases studied and the variety of wearable devices used. These findings offer researchers valuable insights to further explore health care outcomes, validate the impact of concomitant device use, and expand their use to other chronic diseases.

Trial Registration: Open Science Framework Registries (OSF) s4wfm; https://osf.io/s4wfm

### Keywords

### Introduction

#### Background

Noncommunicable diseases, commonly referred to as chronic diseases, are the leading cause of death worldwide, responsible for 41 million deaths annually and accounting for 74% of global mortality [[1](#ref1)]. Aging populations, combined with unhealthy lifestyles such as poor diet and sedentary habits, contribute to a significant rise in chronic disease risk factors, including hypertension, hyperglycemia, and hyperlipidemia [[1](#ref1)]. These diseases are characterized by their long-term nature, slow progression, and need for continuous care and self-management [[2](#ref2)]. Most patients with a chronic disease in primary care have at least one additional chronic condition, making multimorbidity (defined as 2 concomitant chronic diseases) a common challenge that requires integrated, patient-centered care approaches to reduce the treatment burden [[3](#ref3)].

Self-management is a key element of the care plan, and capturing meaningful information to empower individuals with chronic diseases is essential. Technological advancements have paved the way for wearable electronic devices and sensors that empower individuals to manage their health conditions more independently [[4](#ref4),[5](#ref5)]. These innovations support the adherence to disease-related recommendations, such as medication regimens and symptom monitoring, by facilitating real-time monitoring and data collection. Wearable devices operate as data collection tools, transmitting information to software applications for analysis and delivering actionable health updates or notifications [[6](#ref6)]. Key features of these devices include (1) automatic data collection (eg, blood glucose [BG], blood pressure [BP], physical activity, and heart rate); (2) direct transmission of data to the patient and, in some cases, their primary health care providers; and (3) availability of data across multiple platforms (eg, smartwatches, smartphones, and monitors). These systems offer real-time feedback either automatically or via clinician intervention, promoting sustained patient engagement with recommended health behaviors [[4](#ref4)-[6](#ref6)].

This potential for patient empowerment using wearables is observed in several reviews that report positive effects on clinical outcomes, such as glycemic control, and behavioral outcomes, such as symptom self-management [[7](#ref7)-[15](#ref15)]. Systematic reviews have explored the efficacy of wearables in managing diabetes, especially in relation to glycemic control [[8](#ref8)-[15](#ref15)]. BG monitoring devices, a common wearable for diabetes management, exemplify the utility of these technologies in facilitating behavior change and adherence to treatment protocols [[8](#ref8)-[15](#ref15)]. Numerous literature reviews have explored the use of wearable technologies in chronic disease management focusing on specific devices and health outcomes. Existing literature reviews are focused on specific wearable technologies, such as activity trackers [[16](#ref16)-[20](#ref20)] or BG monitors [[8](#ref8),[9](#ref9),[11](#ref11)], or on self-management within particular chronic disease populations, including patients with diabetes [[8](#ref8)-[15](#ref15),[21](#ref21)], cardiovascular diseases [[22](#ref22),[23](#ref23)], and cancer [[7](#ref7)] and cancer survivors [[17](#ref17)], as well as populations at risk of chronic diseases [[19](#ref19),[24](#ref24)].

Several reviews have emphasized the impact of wearables on specific health outcomes, such as increased physical activity [[16](#ref16),[17](#ref17),[19](#ref19),[20](#ref20)]. While these focused reviews offer valuable insights, they often do not capture the broader implications of wearable technology across multiple chronic conditions and diverse health outcomes. Despite the growing body of research, few comprehensive reviews have examined the intersection of various chronic diseases, wearable devices, and the range of health outcomes assessed in intervention studies. A systematic review identified a gap in understanding how wearables influence health outcomes in chronic diseases despite their potential for enhancing self-management [[25](#ref25)]. To address this gap, Mattison et al [[25](#ref25)] conducted a comprehensive review across multiple chronic diseases and outcome measures, highlighting the importance of such an analysis particularly in the context of primary health care.

Building on this work, our review adopted broad objectives aiming to provide an overview of the role of wearables in chronic disease management considering the clinical realities of the variety of devices used and the high prevalence of multimorbidity. A comprehensive analysis of wearable devices across various chronic diseases and health outcomes is essential for future research and patient-centered interventions in primary care settings that will reduce the treatment burden.

#### Objectives

The objectives of this scoping review were to identify and describe (1) the chronic diseases represented in intervention studies, (2) the types or combination of wearables used, and (3) the health or health care outcomes assessed and measured. This scoping review uniquely presents a comprehensive overview of the populations of patients with chronic diseases involved, the wearable devices used, and the specific outcomes targeted in intervention studies.

### Methods

#### Overview

We conducted a scoping review informed by the Joanna Briggs Institute guidelines [[26](#ref26)] and reported our results based on the PRISMA-ScR (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses extension for Scoping Reviews) checklist [[27](#ref27)]. We registered the review protocol in the Open Science Framework Registries [[28](#ref28)]. We followed the population, intervention, comparator, and outcome framework [[29](#ref29)] in establishing the study selection criteria ([Textbox 1](#box1)).

**Inclusion criteria**

**Exclusion criteria**

We included interventions involving at least one wearable device that automatically collected and transmitted data to adult populations with at least one chronic disease in a primary health care setting. The term “wearables” refers to all devices that collect patient data by directly capturing physiological signals, including connected weighing scales, electronic medication pillboxes, and ingestible sensors. Chronic diseases are defined as health conditions that are likely permanent and can be managed in primary health care settings. We excluded studies involving participants who only had a predisposition to develop chronic diseases, hospitalized patients, individuals with acute diseases, patients with active cancer, and cancer survivors.

Our review included randomized controlled trials (RCTs), cohort studies, pretest-posttest studies, observational studies, mixed methods studies, and qualitative studies published between 2012 and 2022. We excluded reviews, surveys, quantitative descriptive studies, metrology or diagnostic studies, protocols or ongoing studies, and conference papers. Moreover, we modified our published protocol [[28](#ref28)] to exclude studies focused solely on the efficacy of connected glucometers that only measure glucose levels as there is already a strong evidence base on this topic [[8](#ref8)-[15](#ref15)].

#### Search Strategy

We (MPG and MS) developed search strategies in the MEDLINE (Ovid) and Web of Science (Science Citation Index Expanded, Social Sciences Citation Index, Arts and Humanities Citation Index, and Emerging Sources Citation Index) databases with an experienced librarian (MCL) to identify sources published between January 1, 2012, and June 30, 2022. The search strategies can be found in [Multimedia Appendix 1](#app1).

#### Data Collection

We imported all references on June 30, 2022, to the web-based collaborative tool Covidence (Veritas Health Innovation) [[30](#ref30)] and removed duplicates manually and using the automated function in Covidence before screening. We conducted a calibration exercise with the reviewers (Victoria Bureau Lagarde, EA, WS, SO, Rouwayda Elouni, and SA) and 2 senior researchers (MPG and MS) using a sample of 10 sources to ensure consistency in the application of the inclusion and exclusion criteria before the full screening process. At the first *title and abstract* screening phase, 8 reviewers (Victoria Bureau Lagarde, EA, WS, SO, MPG, MS, Rouwayda Elouni, and SA) independently assessed the titles and abstracts of the sources by applying the inclusion and exclusion criteria. All titles and abstracts underwent dual screening. Discrepancies in decisions were resolved through team consensus. At the *full-text review* stage, we searched and obtained all missing full texts of the selected references and imported them into Covidence. We conducted another calibration exercise with 10 full texts; 7 reviewers (SO, WS, MS, MPG, Victoria Bureau Lagarde, SA, and EA) independently applied the same selection criteria, and all exclusion motives were recorded in Covidence. All full texts underwent dual screening, and any discrepancies were also resolved through team consensus. From the included texts to extract, we tagged those related to the same studies and combined them for extraction. We also performed backward hand searching [[31](#ref31)] for relevant articles in the bibliographies of the included studies and added them to Covidence for screening and extraction.

#### Data Extraction

Following the Joanna Briggs Institute recommendations [[32](#ref32)], we developed a grid to extract relevant data from the included studies. We tested this grid in a meeting with reviewers (SO, WS, and EA) and 2 senior researchers (MPG and MS). The data extraction of each included study was performed once by 3 different reviewers (SO, WS, and EA) and validated by at least one senior researcher (MPG or MS). In Covidence, we extracted general information (eg, title of the paper, year of publication, lead author, country, and potential author conflicts of interest), methods (eg, RCT, nonrandomized experimental study, cohort study, pretest-posttest study, mixed methods study, qualitative research, or economic evaluation), intervention data (duration of the intervention and follow-up), setting data (where patients were sourced from), participant data (eg, target population, chronic disease or chronic diseases, inclusion and exclusion criteria, sampling methods, recruitment methods, and methods of allocation to the intervention group), total number of participants included in the analysis sampling (at the end of the study), participant baseline characteristics, process of the intervention (eg, wearable devices, intervention administration, type of blinding, comparator administration, and control group description), all outcomes measured and how they were measured, and intervention effects or results.

#### Quality Assessment

We imported the assessment questions from the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool (MMAT) [[33](#ref33)] into Covidence. According to the MMAT, each study is assigned a score that ranges from 0 to 5 stars, with 5 stars signifying the highest quality and reflecting the rigor of the study’s methodology. The quality of each included study was assessed once by 3 different reviewers (SO, WS, and EA), with each study’s score validated by at least one senior researcher (MPG or MS). Discrepancies were resolved through consensus among the reviewers and a senior researcher.

#### Data Synthesis

We structured our analysis according to chronic disease clusters; types and combination of wearable devices used; categories of health and health care system outcomes; and reported effects in terms of positive (reported in the studies as statistically significant), neutral, or negative. We synthesized data using tables explained through a narrative approach. We classified the outcomes reported in the intervention studies into 5 categories. The first category involves clinical outcomes, including indicators measured, such as glucose levels (glycated hemoglobin [HbA1c]), BP, heart rate, weight, BMI, sleep time and quality, and pain intensity. The second category includes behavioral outcomes, such as physical activity levels, activation, motivation, self-efficacy, and disease-related knowledge. The third category involves patient technology experience, such as satisfaction, use, adherence, and engagement. The fourth category includes holistic and biopsychosocial outcomes, such as health status, health-related quality of life (QoL), physical well-being, anxiety, and depression. The fifth category involves the health care system, such as effectiveness, cost-effectiveness, admission or readmission to hospital, and emergency department visits.

### Results

#### Overview

After the removal of 1987 duplicates (n=1971, 99.19% through Covidence and n=16, 0.81% manually), we screened 4540 titles and abstracts. Of the remaining 304 references after exclusion, we excluded 215 (70.7%) full texts and included 89 (29.3%). Of these 89 texts, we tagged 10 (11%) [[34](#ref34)-[43](#ref43)] related to same intervention studies and combined them for extraction, resulting in 79 included studies. We also hand searched and found 8 relevant articles [[44](#ref44)-[51](#ref51)] related to the included studies, totaling 97 references: 10 (10%) combined articles [[34](#ref34)-[43](#ref43)], 8 (8%) hand searched articles [[44](#ref44)-[51](#ref51)], and 79 (81%) main articles of the included studies [[52](#ref52)-[130](#ref130)]. The PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) 2020 flow diagram [[131](#ref131)] of the study inclusion process can be consulted in [Figure 1](#figure1).

![](https://asset.jmir.pub/assets/fa363473ddda31bb4d54fd944ae3f777.png)

#### Characteristics of the Included Studies

Of the 79 studies included, some were published in 2 or even 3 scientific articles. As a result, the number of references (n=97) is higher than the number of studies (n=79). The 97 references [[34](#ref34)-[130](#ref130)] of the 79 included studies [[52](#ref52)-[130](#ref130)] can be found in [Multimedia Appendix 2](#app2). Most studies (45/79, 57%) were conducted in North America (39/45, 87% in the United States and 6/45, 13% in Canada), followed by 9% (7/79) conducted only in the United Kingdom, 5% (4/79) only in the Republic of Korea, 5% (4/79) only in China (one of them was conducted in Hong Kong), 4% (3/79) only in the Netherlands (3/79, and 4% (3/79) only in Spain. 5% (4/79) of the studies were conducted in 2 or more countries, and 11% (9/79) were conducted in other single countries.

We used the MMAT [[33](#ref33)] to assess the methodological quality of the included studies. No studies were excluded based on MMAT scores accordingly with the aim of our scoping review to identify and describe existing knowledge [[26](#ref26)]. The MMAT scores of the 79 studies [[52](#ref52)-[130](#ref130)] can be consulted in [Multimedia Appendix 3](#app3) [[34](#ref34)-[130](#ref130)]. A total of 58% (46/79) of the studies used an RCT design. Most RCTs (20/46, 43%) obtained a score of 4 stars, 26% (12/46) obtained a score of 3 stars, 24% (11/46) obtained a score of 2 stars, and 7% (3/46) obtained a score of 1 star. Per the study context, an RCT could not have 5 stars due to unfeasible patient blinding.

In [Table 1](#table1), we structure our results according to 9 chronic disease clusters: diabetes (18/79, 23% of the studies), heart failure (13/79, 16% of the studies), other cardiovascular conditions (10/79, 13% of the studies), hypertension (8/79, 10% of the studies), multimorbidity and other combinations of chronic conditions (8/79, 10% of the studies), chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD; 8/79, 10% of the studies), chronic pain (6/79, 8% of the studies), musculoskeletal conditions (6/79, 8% of the studies), and asthma (2/79, 3% of the studies).

We also structured our results according to the technologies used. The most frequent were wearable activity trackers (WATs; eg, a smartwatch or a device worn at the chest, waist built-in accelerometers, or connected pedometers). We found WATs in 53% (42/79) of the studies, BP monitors in 35% (28/79) of the studies, weighing scales in 25% (20/79) of the studies, BG monitors in 20% (16/79) of the studies, and electrocardiogram (ECG) monitors/devices in 10% (8/79) of the included studies. A total of 61% (48/79) of the intervention studies used a single wearable (or smart health) device, whereas 39% (31/79) of the studies used ≥2 devices.

In [Table 2](#table2), we structure our results based on the types or combinations of wearables used. [Table 2](#table2) provides an overview of the outcomes measured for each wearable or combination of wearables along with the reported effects. There are 2 points that require clarification. First, the study designs and sample sizes are not presented, but this information is available in [Multimedia Appendix 3](#app3) [[52](#ref52)-[130](#ref130)]. Second, confounding factors such as multicomponent behavioral interventions are not considered. Full details of the sources used for [Table 2](#table2) can also be found in [Multimedia Appendix 3](#app3).

--- SOURCE 5: Wearable biosensors in modern healthcare: Emerging ... ---

ABSTRACT
--------

Wearable biosensor systems represent a significant breakthrough in the life sciences, offering real-time monitoring and quantitative assessment of various human health parameters. The escalating demand for continuous and immediate surveillance of both acute and chronic conditions, compounded by constraints in clinical infrastructure, has propelled extensive investigation into wearable biosensors. These systems have emerged as pivotal tools for furnishing physiological insights and facilitating non-invasive monitoring. They monitor various analytes through the analysis of sweat, epidermal, Interstitial fluids, breath and tears, and easily incorporated into flexible substrates ensuring long-term wear and continuous monitoring. However, these biosensors penetrate deeper into the body's fluid networks, providing a systemic approach to continuous monitoring of biomarkers, which is particularly useful for individuals with metabolic disorders. This comprehensive review explores recent trends and diverse applications of wearable biosensors. These applications empower individuals to dynamically assess their well-being, enabling informed healthcare decisions tailored to individual needs. The convergence of these approaches in wearable biosensors highlights a comprehensive approach to understand individual health. This integration holds the promise of transforming healthcare practice by providing continuous observance and remote monitoring. The result has been a paradigm shift towards active, personalized health care, leading to improved health outcomes and improved quality of life

1. Introduction
---------------

The increasing focus on biosensor advancement is attracting significant interest in biomedicine and healthcare sectors, primarily because of their extensive application in clinical practice, pharmacy, and routine healthcare monitoring [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0001),[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0002)]. A biosensor is a combined receptor-transducer analytical device designed to detect alterations in biological processes and convert them into electrical signals[2]. They encompass a biological sensing component (enzymes, nucleic acids, proteins, etc.) and a physicochemical transducer that converts chemical reactions into discernible electrical signals correlating with the concentration of the analyte [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0003)] as shown in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#fig0001).

![Image 12: Fig 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666831925000888-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (660KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666831925000888-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (660KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666831925000888-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Schematic Representation of Conventional Biosensors comprising of distinct Bioreceptors and different type of Transducers**.**

The advantages of biosensors include their cost-effectiveness, compact size, swift and straightforward operation, as well as superior selectivity and sensitivity. One notable example is the glucose oxidase biosensor utilized by individuals with diabetes to monitor blood sugar levels [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0004)]. However, the routine monitoring of blood sugar levels depends significantly on in-person appointments, manual examinations, and treatment methods that involve substantial human interaction [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0005),[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0006)]. Also, these biosensors are often crafted from rigid materials, and rely on invasive blood extraction at specific intervals limiting their comfort and suitability for extended wear [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0007)]. In contrast, the emergence of wearable biosensors enables the detection and monitoring of biomarkers and therapeutic substances from bodily fluids (tears, sweat, interstitial fluid, and saliva) in a minimally invasive or noninvasive and continuous manner [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0008)]. They are compact electronic gadgets that incorporate sensors either onto or within the human body, taking forms such as eyeglasses, smart watches, tattoos, gloves, clothing, or implants facilitating in vivo sensing, data recording, and computation using mobile or portable devices [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0009),[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0010)]. They offer vital insights into the wearer's current condition and are poised to become a compelling option due to their notable attributes such as affordability, portability, sensitivity, timely diagnosis, and management of medical conditions, especially for managing chronic diseases [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0011)]. Wearable technologies have undergone progressive development, combining multiplexed biosensing, microfluidic sampling, and transport systems with flexible materials and body attachments to enhance wearability and user convenience. In the evolution of wearable biosensors, the incorporation of sensors to detect a wide range of biomarkers poses a significant challenge, necessitating continuous advancements in sensing technology. Electrochemical-based sensors stand out among various types of biosensors due to their advantages in ease of construction, heightened sensitivity, rapid response, and low power consumption. The constitution of wearable sensors heavily relies on sensing electrodes, predominantly employing the electrochemical approach. However, challenges persist regarding critical aspects like functional materials and manufacturing techniques employed in the fabrication of sensing electrodes, underscoring the need for improvement to enhance the performance of wearable biochemical sensors [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0009),[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0012)]. Currently, Internet of Things (IoT) is a highly intriguing subject that offers solutions to overcome these constraints in multiple ways. Certain features, notably the inclusion of wireless power and data transmission capabilities, have markedly heightened the demand for wearable biosensors in the context of remote monitoring and Internet of Things (IoT)-driven sensing applications. Wearable devices, as part of the Internet of Things (IoT), have been developed to assist individuals in receiving appropriate medical care. These smart devices are connected to the internet to collect and transmit data, as well as receive information to facilitate informed decisions, evolving from simple accessories to more specialized and practical utilities. Additionally, these smart wearables can interface with other device groups, such as smartphones, for communication and computational purposes [[[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0013), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0014), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0015)]. Furthermore, these platforms exhibit outstanding attributes, including exceptional flexibility and stretchability, coupled with remarkable sensitivity, enabling them to discern a broad spectrum of signals with precision. However, further studies, including cohort validation and performance evaluation, are necessary to establish the clinical reliability and acceptance of wearable biosensors. In recent times, considerable attention has been directed towards developing wearable sensors, reflecting a growing focus on identifying diverse biomarkers that influence health. With advancements in miniaturized devices and technologies such as microelectronics and wireless communication, wearable biochemical sensors have become deeply integrated into our daily lives. Nevertheless, further development is necessary for the future [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0016),[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0017)].

This comprehensive review critically explores the adaptation of wearable sensors for detecting a variety of analytes, including body fluids. The paper highlights the application of wearable biosensors in clinical practice and healthcare monitoring. It delves into previously conducted research, elucidating the underlying working principles, fabrication methodologies, and the utilization of flexible electrochemical biosensors incorporating nanoparticles and bio receptors for precise analyte detection. It explores the principles underlying biosensors, highlighting their role in detecting and converting biological processes into electrical signals. The paper discusses the advantages of biosensors, such as cost-effectiveness and superior sensitivity, as well as their limitations, including invasiveness and rigidity. A key novelty of this review lies in its systematic classification of wearable biosensing technologies based on the specific biological secretions, namely tears, sweat, epidermal fluids, interstitial fluid (ISF), and exhaled breath. By adopting this secretion-specific framework, the review provides a unique perspective on the design considerations, analytical performance, and application-specific challenges associated with each fluid type. Moreover, it highlights the emergence of next-generation wearable biosensors capable of enabling continuous, non-invasive monitoring, thereby paving the way for decentralized and proactive healthcare models. The review concludes by outlining future research directions, advocating for the development of biocompatible materials, miniaturized power sources, and multi-analyte detection platforms. By addressing both current capabilities and unmet needs, this work provides a valuable roadmap for advancing wearable biosensor technology toward robust, market-ready solutions.

2. Wearable Biosensors: a holistic approach to monitoring health
----------------------------------------------------------------

With advancements in sensor and operating system hardware technology, wearable devices have seen a gradual expansion of functionality and an improvement in the accuracy of physiological indicators. These sensors are now characterized by their increased precision, continuous monitoring capabilities, and enhanced comfort, making significant contributions to personalized healthcare [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0018)]. Their primary purpose is to assist users in monitoring their own health and providing timely alerts to those in need. Wearable sensors offer the distinct advantage of enabling users to engage in scientific health monitoring at any time and in any location. This eliminates the need for frequent clinic visits and allows users to save valuable time by wearing portable sensors.

The benefits of wearable sensors are particularly notable for users who make use of a variety of portable sensors. For individuals with chronic illnesses, wearable devices have the potential to reduce the frequency of hospitalizations. By allowing users to adjust their daily routines and diets based on device data, these devices can help in timely health management, ultimately leading to cost savings on expensive treatments. Wearable devices can also replace traditional medical equipment like ECG gadgets and defibrillators by providing precise measurements of body temperature and heart health, resulting in potential cost savings for hospitals [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0019)].

Additionally, wearable devices empower patients to take better control of their lives and health. They offer real-time insights into an individual's current physical condition, which can be invaluable in ensuring timely hospitalization. As people increasingly prioritize their health with improving living standards, there has been a surge of interest in heart rate [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0020)], blood oxygen monitoring [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0021),[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0022)], sports and fitness, and related equipment [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0023)].

In recent years, the landscape of wearable technology has undergone a significant transformation. Researchers have shifted their focus from tracking regular exercise routines to addressing pressing healthcare challenges, such as diabetic patient management and geriatric remote monitoring [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0016)]. To tackle these complex issues, scientists have concentrated on the development of wearable biosensors, which incorporate biometric elements into sensor operations, including enzymes, antibodies, cell receptors, or organelles [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0018)]. Based on that a schematic representation of latest wearable sensors technology is given in [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#fig0002).

![Image 13: Fig 2](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666831925000888-gr2.jpg)

Fig. 2. Schematic Representation of Wearable Sensor Systems Utilizing Various Biological Fluids.

Various biological fluids found in the human body, including tears, sweat, interstitial fluid (ISF), epidermal fluids, and breath, serve as valuable sources for developing devices that enable early disease detection [[[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0024), [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0025), [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0026), [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0027), [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0028)]. [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#tbl0001) compiles various biological fluid used for the development of wearable biosensors along with their advantages and limitations.

Table 1. Comparison of Various Biological Fluids for Wearable Biosensing Applications.

| Biological Fluid | Key Source | Accessibility | Biomarker Accuracy | Analytical Complexity | Advantages | Limitations |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Tear** | Lachrymal Glands | Moderate (via eye-wearable devices) | High – due to direct diffusion of analytes from blood | Low – streamlined detection of proteins, electrolytes, and metabolites | High precision and specificity, similar composition to blood | Limited volume, collection can be challenging without irritation |
| **Sweat** | Eccrine and apocrine sweat glands (via skin) | High – readily available across the body surface | Moderate – concentrations can vary based on activity and hydration | Moderate – influenced by sweat rate, evaporation, and contamination | Non-invasive, widely accessible, suitable for continuous monitoring | Biomarker concentration variability, potential environmental interferences |
| **ISF** | Between skin cells and capillaries | Moderate – accessed via microneedles or skin-permeable patches | High – closely reflects blood analyte levels | Moderate to High – requires minimally invasive access tools | High biomarker relevance; stable composition | Requires specialized access methods; slower sample replenishment |
| **Epidermal** | Outer skin layers | Low – small quantities | Low to Moderate | High – complex matrix and low volume | Potential for superficial, non-invasive sampling | Low fluid volume; difficult to isolate specific analytes |
| **Breath** | Lungs (exhaled air) | High – easy collection | Low to Moderate – depends on target gases (e.g., VOCs) | High – complex and variable due to environmental and physiological factors | Non-invasive; continuous sampling possible | Variability due to diet, environment, and metabolism |

Among the various types of wearable biosensors, skin-worn biosensor devices have garnered significant attention, primarily because the epidermis covers a substantial portion of the human body [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0029)]. These devices rely on collecting perspiration from the skin's surface or sampling interstitial fluid, facilitating the flow of biofluids across the biosensor surface. The widespread distribution of sweat glands across the body makes perspiration the most readily accessible biofluid for chemical sensing applications. As a result, these biosensor devices offer a promising avenue for efficient and non-invasive disease monitoring [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0030),[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0031)]. These sensors can be attached to the body through various means, including standard clothing, watches, mobile phones, and other everyday items integrated with sensors. Flexible sensors have emerged as a promising innovation, with advances in printed electronics and materials enabling tiny sensors to be used as skin patches. These biosensors are capable of measuring skin conductance, heart rate, body temperature, and detecting changes in pH [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0032),[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0033)], glucose [[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0034)], and salt levels [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0016),[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0035),[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0036)] within the human body. [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#tbl0001) represents a comparison between various biological fluids used for the development of wearable biosensor systems with emphasis upon their advantages and limitations.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Wearable biosensors provide continuous monitoring for chronic diseases like diabetes and heart conditions, enabling tailored interventions and proactive health management.
These devices facilitate real-time monitoring of vital signs such as heart rate, glucose levels, and blood pressure, ensuring timely alerts and interventions.
Wearable biosensors support preventive care by tracking lifestyle metrics, helping users adopt healthier behaviors, and reducing the risk of chronic disease progression.
